Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: Level 1. Player is the magenta object. RIGHT moves player rightward.
- Goal model: Navigate player to colored squares on the right side.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move player. MOUSE and ACTION7 available.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update it. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The player boundary didn't change between the two RIGHT actions - the player stayed at [[36,11]...]. 
# This means RIGHT might not be moving the player, or it moves something else.
# Let me check what actually changed by looking at all nodes

before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

# Compare node counts and boundaries
print("Before nodes: %d" % len(before['nodes']))
print("After nodes: %d" % len(after['nodes']))

# Check if any node boundaries changed
for i, (bn, an) in enumerate(zip(before['nodes'], after['nodes'])):
    if bn['boundary'] != an['boundary']:
        print("Node %d changed: %s -> %s" % (i, bn['boundary'], an['boundary']))

# Let me also check the ascii to see what changed
print("ASCII region around player:
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for r in range(35, 43):
    print("Row %d: %s" % (r, lines[r]))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{
  "world_model": "Level 1. Player is the magenta object at ~row 36-41, col 11-16. RIGHT doesn't seem to move the player. Need to understand what actually changes.",
  "goal_model": "Navigate player to colored squares on the right side.",
  "action_model": "UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT may not move player as expected. Need to test other actions.",
  "recent_findings": ["RIGHT action executed but player boundary unchanged"],
  "open_questions": ["What does RIGHT actually change when I press RIGHT?", "How do I move the player?"],
  "current_plan": "Check what changed, try different actions",
  "cross_level_notes": ""
}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# I see! The player (M magenta) is the magenta object at rows 36-41, cols 11-16.
# When I press RIGHT, the gray/white trail (g/w pattern) extends to the right!
# The trail pattern: gwwgwwgwwgwwgwwgww - it's a path being drawn!
# The dark area boundary (node 1) also expanded rightward.
# So RIGHT extends the path/trail to the right from the player position.
# The colored squares on the right are colored squares (R, b, N) on right side at rows 19-22, 25-28, 31-34)
# Bottom panel has M, R, N, b) are in the bottom panel (rows 54-63)
# Goal: probably to reach the colored squares by extending the trail to match the bottom panel order: M, R, N, b
# Let me try pressing ACTION7 or MOUSE to interact with the colored squares

# Let me check the full ascii to understand the layout better
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for r in range(18, 24):
    print("Row %d: %s" % (r, lines[r]))
print("---")
for r in range(56, 62):
    print("Row %d: %s" % (r, lines[r]))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{
  "world_model": "Level 1. Player is magenta object draws a trail when moving. RIGHT extends the trail to the right. The trail has a pattern of g/w alternating. Colored squares (R, b, N) on the right side. Bottom panel shows M, R, N, b in bottom panel. Goal unclear.",
  "goal_model": "Probably to extend the trail to reach colored squares in the order shown in the bottom panel.",
  "action_model": "UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT extend the trail in that direction. MOUSE and ACTION7 available.",
  "recent_findings": ["RIGHT extends the trail to the right from the player position"],
  "open_questions": ["How to interact with colored squares?"],
  "current_plan": "Try extending trail to reach colored squares",
  "cross_level_notes": ""
}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
